speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eva Weiss, tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mrs. Weiss, when did Edith die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June 30, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you go to Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assigned to go to Sweden two days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: First we went to Malmo, which was a quarantine, and I think we were only there about three weeks. And then they assigned Hungarian girls, assigned us to called Loka Brunn. It was a beautiful summer resort, gorgeous place, and I had-- I-- the story was that it was King Gustav's summer resort which he turned over to the Red Cross just for that. So in Sweden, too, sort of they kept you-- if Czechoslovakia was in a different camp, Hungarians, a different, and a Polish survivor who was transferred to Sweden was in a different camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we spent in there, Loka Brunn-- and of course, I was just with Magda by that time. And not knowing who's alive, I didn't want to live. And I got to Sweden, I did not move off my bed-- well, it was actually a beautiful bungalow. I even showed you the picture. I have the picture of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had our private bed. I didn't want to get off the bed for two weeks. And finally, I think, a Hungarian man-- I heard that he was a rabbi-- he came by, and he started to preach to me. He was going to see if you're sick, somebody's sick or not. And but the thing was-- I have to recall back, jump back to Bergen-Belsen. When she died, they wanted to take me off the transport. I was not going to Sweden. I don't know where I was going. And my cousin carried on. She says, no, I'm sad. I'm going home. If I'm going, I'm going home. I'm going to find my brother, thinking maybe even my mother. I mean, you were still dreaming, and hoping, and, and, and, and I don't know what we were thinking. And Magda says, no, you're not. Your two brothers are not going to take care of a young girl. You're going to America. But I did not know what the name of my American relatives. I didn't know address, like my sister remembered, because she was corresponding with them. So when she died, everything died. I knew the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're in Sweden already, and they're constantly coming to take dates of the survivors, especially while we were in a quarantine three weeks, that was the program from day and night. They were nourishing us nice, then taking care of us, and all this. But they wanted to transport me to Israel, and for some reason I said no. I had so many people around me. No, no. By that time, I'm resigned that really I'm going to America, but I don't know nothing but the names. So every day approached us, where you going? They wanted to assign, because you-- this was a temporary situation, you know, for months. They didn't want-- Sweden didn't want all the people to stay there forever either. So a lot of them went to Israel. My other cousin who was-- she signed herself as Czechoslovakian because a Czechoslovakian transport, for some reason, went out of Bergen-Belsen faster than we did, the Hungarians. And of course, she had no sister to hold her back. I was taking care of my sister, so I didn't care when I'm going to be transferred as long as I'm with her. So my cousin Magda would put the-- we were watched and cared in there in, in Germany in that stage because we were already treated nice by the English, and nurses would come every day and check our fever. So my cousin would put my temp, temp thermometer under a hot water. One would stay outside the hall to see if they coming because I was a child. I was under 18. So first they had to take care of me. So we finally cried to the English doctor and said, if-- you know, I just lost my sister and if you don't take me to Sweden, then I just want to die. OK. Well, you were in-- let's go back to Loka Brunn. So you were in Loka Brunn in Sweden--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Loka Brunn, Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out whether or not any siblings survived, and how did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by that time, we're in that July, August, and you start-- you start talking to the next girls and socialize, who was assigned to the same room. Happens that she really came to Detroit with us. And we start talking, and she's already a big shot showing off that her two brothers in Detroit, and she had addresses. She was a married lady. She even had a child, I think, but she's the survivor. And we're assigned to the same, same building, the same bungalow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to her, I says, Rose, do me a favor. Let me put in your letter a note, and I write down my uncle and Louis Fish. I knew the names. Paula Fish, Louis Fish, my uncle. My cousin is Irene. I didn't know her married name. And Margaret, and I knew her husband, Dr. [INAUDIBLE] in Detroit. And I knew the name of the street, Wager. That's it. So I-- and Eva Lefkowicz from Olaszliszka looking for their aunts in Detroit. Sure enough, I put that note in her letter. Her friend, because Hungarians knew each other in here, too, OK, way back. Her sister-in-law and my first cousin-- the one in here, Irene, who's deceased by now-- they knew each other. This girl calls her up and say, Irene, do you have a cousin Lefkowicz, Eva? And she screamed. She says, yes. Of course, they didn't know me, because when my aunt came to this country, that's when I was born, 1928. And but the other cousin, Margaret, who was home, she knew me as a 10-year-old little whiny kid who was always running after her. And she gets the note, and, and I write to her that I'm looking for-- we are liberated, we're alive, we are-- we even wrote to you from Bergen-Belsen. Do you know, she went to the Lynnwood Post Office and our letter from my sister was there? And she thought that I'm in Sweden with my-- and I write that I'm in Sweden, and I don't know the rest of the family, who's alive or who not, and please, I would like to come to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on Friday night, already by that time, we go to swim in there and we get into that group of socializing. They're giving us-- showing movies, teaching us English, teaching us Swedish, and you have a sort of a normal life. And on Friday night-- I never forget-- we're in line. And we had a huge dining room in there too, but it wasn't, you know, nothing like camp. It was like-- it's really a summer resort, beautiful dining room. And I'm in line, and I think I'm having soup or something, and a good looking Swedish [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], we called him. He comes-- his name was Gustav too-- and he calls out, Eva Lefkowicz, telegram. And I dropped the plate, and I knew that was from America. And the telegram says, stay where you are. We're bringing you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you find your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from through her, from America getting the corresponding letters-- and I think that was one of the happiest days of my life-- and I opened a letter from her where already my uncle who went before us in Debrecen-- he don't ever even left Hungary. He was just in hiding. He already contacted, and he found my two brothers came back from labor camp. And I opened a letter, and the name Bela and Laszlo-- Laci-- and I knew they're alive. So and that time, I don't even know if I was able to write to Hungary. I don't think so, because Hungary was already occupied by the Russians after that. And I just knew that they're alive. And then later on, my brother, when he went to Israel-- Bela-- he wrote to me. I guess, I don't even know through who I got address or what. But already I was in Sweden nine months to get a visa for us to come. And of course, my aunt and uncle did not know about my Magda cousin, who was my father's side, the relation. They knew of the family, I mean, they knew the family and all that. And I wrote to them, if she can't come out, then I'm not coming either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and Magda came out to the United States. What date did you emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in April 8 to New York, and we came with a luxurious boat, regular visa, regular tickets, in 1946, April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- did you go immediately to Detroit after New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, immediately. My mother's cousin in New York who lived in Long Island waited for us with a big bouquet, and when we arrived-- See, I have to tell you first, that when we were in Sweden, that nine months, every single week, or at least every second week, I would get from one cousin $20, $25 from the other one, and [INAUDIBLE] whatever. And my uncle and aunt were older. My uncle, when I came out, had his first heart attack, and he just made-- he worked for his brother. But his brothers were a big, large family who's no relation to me, just know. They just took us into the center of their life. They're the one who send the money for the ticket, which was $300 those days-- it was a lot of money-- for each of us, and then every week.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we did, the minute we had this [INAUDIBLE] of money, we were already rich. We bought clothes for ourself in a regular store, and my cousin would, you know, sew. And then first thing, with the first $20, we went-- end of the town was a little bit of a, like a little coffee shop and a restaurant, because this was like a summer place, like any summer-- a small place. And we went, with that first $20 to the bank and cashed it, and bought a big salami, a whole whatchamacallit, you know, a whole piece, a big piece of salami, and their Swedish bread, which happened to be very tasty, but their food was always sweet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the way walking back to our bungalow, we just chunked it and ate it like pigs. So we actually were fed good, and then we started to buy clothes. So when we got off the train, I don't know what my cousin expected, but we had normal Western clothes already from Sweden, you know, hat, purse, coat, everything, except no makeup. My cousin still wouldn't let me put nothing on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you went from New York to Detroit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. The next morning-- the next day they put us to the train to Detroit, and I'm in Detroit since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Detroit, what did you do? Did you get a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived to Detroit, right away, about two, three-- two weeks, I think-- my uncles, who I call uncle. They are. But Uncle Dave had a store in downtown, and he would take me-- the only-- the school was open daytime for English was all the way across from the city hall, way down downtown. In those days, they lived on Dexter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would take-- he would take me for at least a week. And after that, every morning, showed up at my aunt's house, picked me up, and took me to school. And Magda just stayed around the house, you know, with my aunt because she didn't want to go in a day school. I don't even know why, but she didn't. I wanted. So after a week or so, I said to my uncle, oh no, I'm big enough, and I understand enough that I'm going to go take the bus on myself alone. So sure enough, I took the bus, and I'm going on a bus, going, so I thought I'm already downtown. And I get off on Grand Boulevard. [LAUGHS] After that, I learned how to get there by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to school all that summer. And my cousin, who is in Caracas now-- see, the family came together and it was Pesach. A week later, it was Passover. So my cousin from Washington, a doctor and a wife, everybody, comes to meet us. And the friend of theirs brother from Caracas was visiting, and of course, Magda was already 24, 25, so I'm just still a kid. My 18th birthday was in America. And they introduce him to this certain boy, who she, of course, marries. By September she was married. So she's gone. I still live with my aunt. And my first job was September. After, you know, went all summer schools, September I go out to work because I realize that I have to make some money. And my first job was in [INAUDIBLE] Chocolate, packing chocolate, which was marvelous, because we could eat as much as you wanted, and we were hungry and I always loved chocolate. And I think I made $20. And then I met the girls. And you would meet-- you would-- and then I started to go. Soon as I started to work, I started to go to Central High, and that was the gathering for all the [INAUDIBLE], all the Europeans who were coming out-- Germans, Hungarian, Polish, all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met a girl who came just a month before I did. So actually when we came, we were the first. She was the first in January, but-- and we were the one who came to Detroit, the first Hungarians in the whole Hungarian community. And then my uncle and all of them belonged to [INAUDIBLE] [? Mosch. ?] And I was write up, and I was even-- Carol has that paper interviewed. I think was you know? And just, you know, a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was wonderful to us. To us, everybody was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about meeting your husband and when you married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was here in about four weeks, and a family knew each other, again. He's on-- he just came back from the Army after four years. And he's a-- my uncle and his uncle were just closest friend and pals. And evidently my uncle told him, you're not-- because he was 26 and ready to get married and the war is over so they wanted he should marry. So he said, no, you're not marrying anybody till my niece comes out, and he waited for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was here like four weeks-- no, yeah, it was longer. I was here about two months when they finally, at my aunt's house, bring him with his pal. He didn't know Hungarian at all, and my English was still poor. And his friend was there-- translator-- and they took me out to the restaurant, and talking, and I did not like the way he was talking. Because he was American soldier in Bergen-Belsen, and he saw a lot of thing. And he saw how wild things were, which, of course, that part I didn't know because I was occupied with my sister constantly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had no respect for any of the survivors, and I was terribly insulted, furious with him. I came home, I told my cousin, you fixed me up with somebody like that you want me to meet. The poor guy didn't open his mouth so he was-- because he was other side of-- he was at the Persian Gulf in his four years in the Army. And this guy was in Germany as American soldier, a Jewish boy. He actually met somebody, and it was a dirty story anyway, and brought it out, and the poor girl, by the time she came out, he didn't want to marry her. So he was a devil, but a handsome, handsome guy. Very handsome guy. We are friends still today. That's the crazy part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, but so you-- so you, you met your husband on this blind date, and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to go out with him. And I just dated from the school, you know, the boys, the German boy, or anybody who we met, and if I liked them in school, which quite a few I know were not [INAUDIBLE]. And a year went by. Following year, spring, he calls me up, just like that. And then already I could, you know, talk to him, and my English was better. And he says, I want you to come out with him and I want you to take me out to a theater. And it was another girl who I knew well who was actually-- bec-- was in a family. And we double date, and from then on, the date was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- how long after that date did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got engaged, I think, in July, and we got married in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: At '47. '47, November 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your life since your marriage. Tell me about your work and about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning when I was very happy to be married, happy to have a home, happy to be on my own. And my cousin, of course, I told you, who lived with us, she already went to Caracas, and I didn't hear from her too much. And I was very hurt by that, and that-- well, there was a reason for that, too, but of course, I didn't know that then. Anyway, and so I was happy, and dating, and doing all the things that the American youngsters do-- go to shows, go to a play. And he was marvelous to me. And I actually, when I found out his life story, I felt sorry for him more because he didn't absolutely not know anything what happened to his family, how they perished. Till today he doesn't know if they went to a concentration camp or not. So when-- you know, when we started really getting serious and talking, I think I felt sorry for him more than for myself, and I started to be happier and contented. In those days, we did, even between us, and then every month more and more girls would come out, you know, to this country and to Detroit. And I-- we always met, like I said, in a Central High, in the school. And if you liked a certain person, you already made a clique. You had your own friends. And that's how we form our group of friends and everything. And when I got married, I felt like a million dollar. I had my little apartment. I had my new furniture, which I want you to see, this is it. It's 48 years-old. And, and I was-- oh, by that time, I was working downtown, and my girlfriend introduced me and she took me to a clothing manufacturers where they made suits and coats, and I did not know how to sew. The boss wanted to fire me because I didn't know how to hold a thimble. And this girl, really, literally taught me how to sew, really, truly. And that's how after that, I wind up going to night school and learned how to make drapes, which I never dreamed those days that I'm going to be in a drapery business. And because you had no money, but I wanted something on a window, so I learned, and I went to [? Hart's ?] and bought fabric and all this, and learned on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have your first child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first? I was 20 years-old, in '49, January, and I was very-- I was scared being pregnant that fast. My husband was delirious because the rumors were very, very horrid that anybody who survived cannot have children. And all these kind of rumors were already in this country, and they heard. And of course, we didn't want to believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I got pregnant, I was married, young, 20, inexperienced. I wanted to be just for myself, live, really, truly. And I came home from the doctor, crying, what am I going to do with a baby? And he was thrilled. And then, of course, after each other, my kids just came, and we were very happy. We didn't have much, but we were happy. Tell me about your-- the names of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my oldest one is Jeffrey, and he's married, thank God. He has a family of three children. He's a dentist. My second one is Stephen, and he is, unfortunately, unmarried, but he has a gorgeous son, a 14-year-old boy, Daniel. And Carol, and she was like from heaven, because I was dying for a little girl. And I had to say that, and when I had her, I think I was delirious for three days, and I thought I was the most genius person on earth that I finally have a daughter I want. So they were loved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren, we got six, thank God. We got Danny. And we got Erica, Emily, Erin. We got Lori and Mark. So my son has three, and my other son has one, and Carol, my daughter, has two. And they were all precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what business have you been in for the last how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last 25, 26 years, my husband was in a meat business working for his uncle. And as the children was growing up, I was restless and I was kind of unsatisfied with life and all. And I said, I'm going into business. And he says, are you crazy? I says, no, I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my girlfriends were already in business in a drapery business, and one day we went out to [INAUDIBLE] visit them. And he inform me that he wants to sell a business, and I said, I'm buying. And my husband looked at it, what do you mean you're buying? You don't know nothing about it. I says, I'll learn. And which I did. I went to work for four weeks for him. By that time, we already signed a deal. Everything was done. I went to my uncle-- I didn't have enough money-- borrowed money from him, and he was willing to give us. And my husband says, what are you going to do? I said, the kids are in school. They were already 13-- older. By the time I bought the business, my son was graduated from high school. He was ready to go to college, and he wanted to go to University of Michigan. That was his dream. And that's that year we moved here, and I said to myself, how are we going to do it? We can't afford that. And I begged him to stay in town. No, he was too good of a student, and he wanted to University of Michigan. So when this opportunity came on, I said, I'm going to buy. So I went into the store. It was Lincoln Park, at Sears Shopping Center, a drapery store. And he was Draped by Star, but after that I changed the name, Five Star, for my ch-- for five of us, for the children. And I had to come up with the name and keep the Star, so I thought this would be just right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to work. And the girls in there, nobody knew that I am owning already the store, just to watch what's going on, how to sell, and how to order, and all that. And my husband still was working for his uncle, so it really helped us. So it was on like for a year, I was running the store. And about four, five weeks later, I let the manager know that they're all working for me and I own the store. And we were very good to them and they were pleased-- oh, God-- pleased to, you know, that that's happening, and there was no disruption and no interruption. And business was as usual, and thank God we managed. And then two years-- not even two years later, I bought a second store because we expanded. By that time, my husband stopped his business, you know, the meat business, and he started to learn this business. And my kids were grown already, and they helped us. We were transferring merchandise from Lincoln Park to Mt. Clemens and back and forth. And then one of the buyers approached me that I should open up in Rochester, that's the upcoming area, and that's where I belong, and for doing customs. And, and I thought I was pretty good, and I knew how to measure and everything was going. But in Lincoln Park was not much of an opportunity for custom. So there we opened up in a brand new shopping center from scratch, a store, and we both worked in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Weiss, today, November 15, 1995, how long have you and your husband been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 48 years. It was last week. Long time. Thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on your life, spending this time reflecting, why do you think it's important to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to tell you, Fran. First I was very hesitant, very reluctant if I'm going to give this interview. I didn't know if I can go through with it. But then when I thought about it, I said, the memory of my sister [CRYING] who suffered and didn't make it. My father evidently suffered and didn't make it and, and all the relatives, and all the six million Jews deserved it. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it important for us to remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want my grandchildren to know that when they hear this, it's not a story like when we read the, the Pesach, when we read the stories. We all just reading it as a story and can't even visualize that it was really that torture and that all this horrible things were happening. And I feel if they're going to see me, especially my grandchildren, they can tell their own children that  this was actually happening, and that was my grandmother who's telling the story. And I hope to God that never, never do we forget this, and never, in any of their lifetime, ever, will be another Holocaust. And if they ever, life will change for them, I just hope that they'll be strong enough, they'll have faith, they'll have belief in themself that they have to fight life. Because life is very unpredictable, and you don't know what's going to happen from next day to another day. And I really, truly hope that this story can help them face up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we Jewish people suffered so much. And when you look around and you're seeing, you know, and Israel happened, and what happened in there, but we still have to survive. There is-- I always thought about it, this is, why me? I mean, this is another thing we never went through it, that for a long time we walked around, all the survivors, guilty. Why me? Why life?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm thinking back, that it had to be a God watching over us, because those things, those little experiences, just what I gave you now, there was nothing. The rest of it-- to survive, to work in this condition, and being hungry and still survive, there had to be somebody bigger than us watching us because normally nobody would survive those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So why was that me? I don't know. I don't know. All I know is that when my sister died, this one rabbi I shortly mentioned to you, he came in and he figured out the date for my yahrzeit-- the memorial day-- in Hebrew for me, and he says, Eva, you must live. There has got to be a purpose. You will be-- you are young. You're going to marry. You'll have a family. And when I think back, that really from that, I have six grandchildren. And just a single kid left in a world, somebody was watching over us. [CRYING] But why was it written that it had to be just only me and my two other brothers? And my brother, poor guy, he was very young when he died in here in Detroit in leukemia. And I don't know. There's no answer for that. Is there anything else that you would like to say before we bring  in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's enough. I just feel that-- I hope that my grandchildren, their children will somehow, when they learn about it, and they're not going to just get it into their head that it's a reading, and they're reading a story, that they will just think about it, and have faith, and not to lose their faith in their Jewishness, because we are the chosen people. I don't know why. But there's something in that, that we are survivors, and we are fighters. And from that Holocaust came Israel. When you think about that, it's a miracle. And it's a miracle even now that they're existing. Because they were broken down people who were immigrating over there. And they picked up their life, and they made a new life, and they made a-- formed a new land, which I hope they will keep for rest of their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Yes, you're going to be in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Weiss, can you tell us who is standing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our middle son, Stephen L. Weiss. He's an attorney. And this is my youngest, Carol Walters, and she's a registered nurse on her own. And she has two children, and her husband is Brad Walters, who's a doctor at Beaumont Emergency. And my son has a gorgeous son, which you will see on the pictures, 14-year-old, first grandchild, Danny. And my oldest one, of course, couldn't make it, which is Jeffrey Weiss. His wife is Sandy and have three children, I told you, Erin, and Lori, and Marky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this handsome man standing behind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind me is my husband who I'm married to for 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sidney.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything that you would like to say to your children who are here, your son and grandchildren who are not here, and to your husband, is there anything that you'd like to say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I would like to say to them just one
